notes.note: Cicero ties his planned sailing to the lunar calendar; the new moon of late April 49 BC gave a fixed earliest date for departure. Ancient mariners watched the moon and season closely, the open sailing year running roughly from spring to autumn.

notes.anchor: you are asking Arcadia of me
notes.note: A Greek proverb meaning to demand the impossible, since Arcadia in the Peloponnese was a byword for backwardness. Charged with reforming his wayward nephew young Quintus, Cicero protests that the task is beyond him.

notes.anchor: buy that lodging from Canuleius for fifty thousand sesterces
notes.note: The sesterce was the standard Roman unit of account; fifty thousand was a modest price for a wayside lodging (deversorium) used to break a journey. Cicero kept a chain of such stopping-places along his usual routes.

notes.anchor: the day for payment was to be the Ides of November
notes.note: The Ides, the 13th or 15th of the month, was the customary day for settling debts and rents at Rome. Setting payment for the Ides of November was thus a routine commercial term.

notes.anchor: The fifteenth day before the Kalends of May
notes.note: The Kalends were the first of the month; counted inclusively backward, this dates the letter to 17 April 49 BC. Cicero often closes a letter with the Roman date in this form.
